name: GeoGuessr Meta
description: Read a round like a pro: driving side, stop signs, license plates, bollards, camera generations, Google car tells, scripts and alphabets, and the famous country metas, distilled from the community's Plonk It playbook.

fields.front: The sun sits clearly in the **northern** sky. Which hemisphere are you in?
fields.extra: Sun in the south means Northern Hemisphere. Between the tropics only trust the sun when it is clearly north or south; in summer months it can flip.

fields.front: Which four European countries drive on the **left**?
fields.back: The United Kingdom, Ireland, Malta, and Cyprus
fields.extra: The Isle of Man and the Channel Islands drive left too. Mediterranean scrub vs Atlantic green tells the island pairs apart.

fields.front: Which African countries with Street View drive on the **right**?
fields.back: Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Rwanda, Tunisia, and Egypt, plus Madagascar and Réunion
fields.extra: Southern and eastern Africa (South Africa, Botswana, Namibia, Lesotho, Eswatini, Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania) all drive on the left.

fields.front: Which East and Southeast Asian countries with Street View drive on the **right**?
fields.back: South Korea, Taiwan, Cambodia, Vietnam, Laos, and the Philippines
fields.extra: Driving left: Japan, Hong Kong, Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, and the whole Indian subcontinent (India, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, Pakistan).

fields.front: Where in the Americas do cars drive on the **left**?
fields.back: Suriname and Guyana on the mainland, plus Caribbean islands like Jamaica, the Bahamas, and the US Virgin Islands
fields.extra: The US Virgin Islands are the only left-driving part of the United States.

fields.front: Empty road, no traffic. How do you still deduce the driving side?
fields.back: Traffic signs stand on the driving side; visible Google car parts (like the rear antenna) hang over your lane; on dirt roads the dust trail blows out behind the car; parked cars keep the steering wheel toward the centre line.
fields.extra: Exception: right-hand-drive Japanese imports are everywhere in eastern Russia.

fields.front: A stop sign reads **ALTO**. Where are you?
fields.back: Mexico or Central America (Guatemala, Costa Rica, Panama)
fields.extra: Panama's ALTO font is noticeably thicker than the others.

fields.front: A stop sign reads **PARE**. Where are you?
fields.back: South America, in both the Spanish- and Portuguese-speaking countries, plus the Dominican Republic and Puerto Rico
fields.extra: So a PARE in the Caribbean does not mean South America; check the architecture and plates.

fields.front: A stop sign reads **ARRÊT**. Where are you?
fields.extra: Signs carrying both STOP and ARRÊT are most common in New Brunswick.

fields.front: A stop sign reads **DUR**. Where are you?
fields.extra: Unique to Turkey.

fields.front: A stop sign reads **BERHENTI**. Where are you?
fields.extra: BERI LALUAN yield signs are equally unique to Malaysia.

fields.front: A stop sign shows a **white hand** instead of any word. Where are you?
fields.extra: No word to read, so it works in every language.

fields.front: A stop sign is written in a loopy Southeast Asian script: Thailand or Laos?
fields.back: Count the characters: Thai stop signs have three, Lao stop signs have two.
fields.extra: Lao script is also curvier, with fewer straight lines than Thai.

fields.front: A long white plate with a **blue strip on the left** edge. What does it tell you?
fields.extra: The strip carries the EU stars above a country code; the plate alone narrows you to Europe, the details narrow the country.

fields.front: Which two European countries use **yellow plates front and rear**?
fields.extra: Luxembourg's are long and yellow with the blue EU strip. The UK is yellow at the rear only.

fields.front: White front plate, **yellow rear plate**. Which country?
fields.extra: Rare pre-2009 French cars also have a yellow rear plate.

fields.front: France and Italy both put blue strips on their plates. What is the tell?
fields.back: Italy: two equally visible blue strips and a much shorter front plate. France: the left strip shows more than the right.
fields.extra: French rear plates were yellow before 2009; you may still meet them in old coverage.

fields.front: A plate has a blue strip on the left **and a yellow strip on the right**. Which country?
fields.extra: Unique to Portugal, but discontinued in 2020, so Generation 4 coverage increasingly shows blue-only plates.

fields.front: Private cars have **yellow plates** in South America. Which country?
fields.extra: Colombian taxis are pastel yellow with white plates; Ecuadorian taxis look similar but carry orange plates.

fields.front: A blurred Latin American plate shows a **black dot** in the middle. Which country?
fields.extra: Argentina's other plate, white with a blue top bar, is shared with Brazil (Mercosur design).

fields.front: A black plate blurs into white sections: **three sections vs two**. Which countries?
fields.extra: Yellow plates on Indonesian roads mean commercial vehicles.

fields.front: Short **yellow plates** on private cars in Southeast Asia. Which country?
fields.extra: Thai private plates are white; Thailand saves yellow for commercial vehicles like taxis.

fields.front: Long white plate at the front, **short yellow plate at the rear**. Which country?
fields.extra: Sri Lanka also has lowcam in its Generation 4 coverage, a second quick confirm.

fields.front: Yellow private plates and **red commercial plates** in the Middle East. Which country?
fields.extra: Oman also pairs yellow outer roadlines with white middle lines.

fields.front: An **all-yellow** plate in the United States. Which state?
fields.extra: Front plates are required in Alaska; Generation 4 also shows a design with a big orange circle.

fields.front: Low resolution, wide circular blurs at the bottom **and in the sky**. Which camera generation?
fields.extra: Reading signs is often hard in Gen 2. The sky blur is what separates it from lowcam.

fields.front: Decent resolution but **dull, unsaturated colours** and a greyish sky. Which camera generation?
fields.extra: The sky looks grey even on sunny days.

fields.front: Crisp, vibrant, slightly **oversaturated** image. Which camera generation?
fields.extra: The default for most modern coverage.

fields.front: A low-res camera with one wide circular blur over the car, no sky blur. The community calls it 'shitcam'. Its main country?
fields.extra: Also common in Nigeria, Cambodia, Lebanon, and Ecuador, and scattered across Europe since summer 2025.

fields.front: Which three countries use **only** a low-mounted camera (lowcam)?
fields.extra: Sri Lanka's Generation 4 is lowcam too. Confirm by looking down: the car blur is noticeably bigger. Uphill views can fake it, and Gen 2's circular blur mimics it.

fields.front: Gen 4 image quality, but the car blur is shaped like a **ladybug**: round with a small bump at the front. Which camera?
fields.extra: Introduced late 2024 and mounted lower than regular Gen 4. Very widespread in the Balkans.

fields.front: A grey pickup truck with a **snorkel** in Generation 4. Which country?
fields.extra: Kenya and Mongolia are the only countries with a snorkel in Gen 4.

fields.front: A big pickup with roof racks, **red side mirrors**, sometimes a tent on top. Which country?
fields.extra: All Mongolian coverage used this truck; tent colour and snorkel direction even narrow the region.

fields.front: Gen 3 roof rack with **black tape** on the front bar and a red rectangle on the hood. Which country?
fields.extra: The famous 'Ghana tape'. Gen 4 Ghana is a grey or white pickup with a front antenna.

fields.front: A **police car** tails the Google car in nearly every round. Which country?
fields.extra: The escort is usually white or black, following a visible Street View pickup.

fields.front: A civilian **follow car** shadows the Google car in North Africa. Which country?
fields.extra: It can be ahead of you or far behind, so pan and zoom. The Google car itself is black with a thin antenna.

fields.front: A Gen 3 car with a visible **right side mirror**. Which country?
fields.extra: Unique to Senegal; rifts in the sky are common in the same coverage.

fields.front: A black car with a **long antenna** across a huge northern country. Which one?
fields.extra: Russian plates are plain white and often unblurred in Gen 3; the region code on the plate's right side pins the federal subject.

fields.front: A Gen 3 **black car with its back visible**. Which Latin American country first?
fields.extra: All of Argentina's Gen 3 used this car; in Latin America it also appears in Uruguay and Peru.

fields.front: The Gen 3 'white car' in Latin America means which three countries?
fields.extra: Other white cars exist (Gen 4, or differently blurred Gen 3), so the meta refers to that specific unblurred Gen 3 car.

fields.front: Roof rack, visible mirrors, and **every round looks like late autumn or winter**. Which country?
fields.extra: The whole country was driven in the winter months: bare trees, drab grass, snow on the ground.

fields.front: Round white bollard with a **pointed top** and a reflector band wrapped all the way around. Which country?
fields.extra: A similar Scottish bollard has a blunt top. Rare French variants have a fully red top.

fields.front: Black-and-white wedge bollard with grey reflectors: Germany or Luxembourg?
fields.back: Count the bolts on the reflector: two means Germany, three means Luxembourg.
fields.extra: German reflectors turn orange next to intersections.

fields.front: White wedge bollard with a **yellow-orange front reflector** and two white dots on the back. Which country?
fields.extra: Germany's lookalike keeps a white or grey front reflector. Portugal's orange reflectors are noticeably darker than Spain's.

fields.front: White bollard with a black top: **dark red** vs **bright red** front reflector?
fields.back: Dark red or grey: Austria. Bright red with a white back reflector: Slovenia (Montenegro is similar).
fields.extra: Austrian bollards often wear a small nipple-shaped black cap.

fields.front: A **yellow bollard** with a white reflector on nearly every rural road. Which country?
fields.extra: Extremely common throughout the country and unique to it.

fields.front: Small cylindrical white bollard on a **black base**. Which country?
fields.extra: Unique to Mexico; a red band near the top usually means Nayarit.

fields.front: A white **bowling-pin shaped** bollard with two red stripes near the top. Which country?
fields.extra: Completely unique to Mongolia.

fields.front: White bollard with a red front reflector: Australia or New Zealand?
fields.back: New Zealand's red reflector wraps around the sides; Australia's stops on the face, with a grey reflector on the back.
fields.extra: Black-and-white striped bollards mean Western Australia; black-and-yellow means Queensland.

fields.front: Square bollard with a pointed top and **black-and-white stripes**. Which country?
fields.extra: Pairs with yellow middle lines to lock in Thailand fast.

fields.front: Wedge bollard with **almost-square reflectors**: red front, white back. Which country?
fields.extra: The squarish reflector shape is the tell against the rest of the Balkans.

fields.front: A small square concrete bollard, white with a **solid red top**. Which country?
fields.extra: Vietnamese sign posts are striped red-and-white, and its road signs have a yellow-orange background, unique in Southeast Asia.

fields.front: Wedge bollard with **double orange reflectors** on the front. Which country?
fields.extra: The back carries a single white reflector. Slovakia's newer bollard copies Hungary's red-reflector wedge but is still rare.

fields.front: **Yellow centre lines** in the Nordics. Which country first?
fields.extra: Norwegian yellow has an orange tinge, and smaller roads use long-dashed outer lines. Careful: older Finnish coverage also shows yellow centre lines, now being phased out.

fields.front: Yellow centre line with white outer lines. Which part of the world is this the default?
fields.extra: Canada leans single yellow, the US double yellow; a lean, not a law.

fields.front: Thailand vs Malaysia from the road paint alone?
fields.back: Thailand paints yellow middle lines; Malaysia is all white, often double white centre lines, unique in Southeast Asia.
fields.extra: Driving side won't help: both drive on the left.

fields.front: **Yellow outer lines** with white centre lines. Which region?
fields.back: The Middle East: Israel, Jordan, the UAE, and Oman
fields.extra: Botswana pairs yellow outer lines with an arid landscape too, but Botswana drives on the left and the Middle East on the right.

fields.front: Yellow middle lines with white outer lines in East Asia. Which country?
fields.extra: Japan's most common pattern is a dashed white middle line, though yellow exists there too. Hangul circles settle any doubt.

fields.front: Yellow and white centre lines painted **together**, on concrete slab roads. Which country?
fields.extra: Almost every paved Philippine road is concrete slabs, and the combined yellow-white centre line is rare anywhere else in East Asia.

fields.front: **Black chevrons with yellow arrows** in Southeast Asia. Which country?
fields.extra: Every other Southeast Asian country flips it: yellow chevrons with black arrows.

fields.front: The only country in the world with **yellow chevrons carrying burgundy-red arrows**?
fields.extra: A microstate-sized clue that instantly ends the round.

fields.front: Chevrons: **black with white arrows** vs **red-and-white**?
fields.back: Black with white arrows: Switzerland. White-on-red or red-on-yellow: Austria.
fields.extra: Slovenia: red arrows on white. Serbia: black arrows on white. Czechia and Slovakia: red arrows on white.

fields.front: Austria vs Slovenia in an alpine toss-up: which sign colour saves you?
fields.extra: Bright red bollard reflectors also point to Slovenia over Austria's dark red.

fields.front: Czechia vs Slovakia by their blue directional signs?
fields.back: Czech signs use large filled white arrows; Slovak signs use small white arrows.
fields.extra: Slovak sign borders sit right at the sign's edge, Czech borders are inset from it.

fields.front: Bilingual signs where one language is written in *italics*. Which country?
fields.back: Ireland: Irish is italicised above the English
fields.extra: Works even when the place names look nothing alike.

fields.front: Å together with **Ä and Ö**
fields.extra: Danish and Norwegian pair Å with Æ and Ø instead.

fields.front: Å together with **Æ and Ø**
fields.extra: Splitting the two on letters alone is hard; lean on plates, bollards, and terrain (Norway is the mountainous one).

fields.front: Only Ä and Ö for accents, with **doubled vowels and consonants** everywhere (aa, ii, kk)
fields.extra: A Uralic language, so it looks nothing like Swedish or Norwegian.

fields.front: **Þ (thorn) and Ð (eth)**
fields.extra: Faroese uses Ð and Ø but never Þ; Icelandic has Ö, never Ø.

fields.front: Ä, Ö, Ü and **ß**
fields.extra: Germany and Austria; Switzerland writes ss instead of ß.

fields.front: Doubled vowels (aa, ee, oo) and the digraph **ij**
fields.extra: The Netherlands and Belgium's Flanders. 'straat' means street.

fields.front: **Ő and Ű**, double acute accents
fields.extra: Unique to Hungarian; expect long words drowning in acutes and umlauts, unrelated to every neighbour.

fields.front: **Ł** with a stroke, plus ą, ę, ż, ź, ś
fields.extra: The struck-through Ł is unique to Polish.

fields.front: Czech or Slovak: which special letters decide it?
fields.back: Ř, Ě, Ů mean Czech. Ľ, Ĺ, Ŕ, Ô, Ä mean Slovak.
fields.extra: Both share acute vowels (á, é, í), which South Slavic languages lack.

fields.front: **Ă, Ș, Ț**: breve and comma hooks
fields.extra: A Romance language among Slavic neighbours; also uses Â and Î. Moldova reads the same.

fields.front: **Dotless ı**, dotted capital İ, plus Ğ and Ş
fields.extra: The dotless ı is essentially unique to Turkish.

fields.front: Latin-script signs show **Č, Ć, Đ, Š, Ž**. Which languages are in play?
fields.back: Croatian, Bosnian, Montenegrin, or Latin-script Serbian
fields.extra: Slovenian trims the set to Č, Š, Ž: no Ć or Đ.

fields.front: Macron vowels **Ā, Ē, Ī** plus hooked consonants Ģ, Ķ, Ļ, Ņ
fields.extra: Macrons are the Baltic tell for Latvia.

fields.front: Hooked vowels **Ą, Ę, Į, Ų** and dotted Ė
fields.extra: Place names love to end in -ai.

fields.front: **Õ** next to Ä, Ö, Ü
fields.extra: Finnish-looking, but Õ settles it. Despite geography it is unrelated to Latvian and Lithuanian.

fields.front: **Ã and Õ** tildes, Ç, and endings like -ção
fields.extra: Spanish restricts the tilde to Ñ and adds ¿ and ¡.

fields.front: **Ñ**, inverted ¿ and ¡
fields.extra: Shared by Spain and Spanish America; pair with plates and driving clues to pick the continent.

fields.front: **Œ**, ç, and circumflex vowels (ê, â, ô)
fields.extra: France, Wallonia, Romandy, Québec, and much of Africa; the Œ ligature is the strongest single tell.

fields.front: Latin letters **stacked with multiple diacritics**, one-syllable words
fields.extra: More diacritics than any other language on Street View.

fields.front: Bilingual signs in Spain full of **K, X, and Z**, with no recognisable Romance words. Which language?
fields.extra: The Basque Country in northern Spain and the French border region; a language isolate related to nothing.

fields.front: Double **LL** everywhere, with W and Y working as vowels. Which language?
fields.extra: Bilingual signs put you in Wales.

fields.front: Blocky syllables full of **circles**
fields.extra: The circles set it apart from Chinese and Japanese instantly.

fields.front: Dense characters mixed with curvy simple ones like **の** and **し**. Which language?
fields.back: Japanese: hiragana and katakana alongside kanji
fields.extra: Characters only, with no curvy kana, means Chinese (Taiwan or Hong Kong on Street View).

fields.front: Thai vs Lao vs Khmer script at a glance?
fields.back: Thai: loops with straight ascenders. Lao: same family but curvier, fewer straight lines. Khmer: the most intricate and pointed.
fields.extra: Countries: Thailand, Laos, Cambodia.

fields.front: A Cyrillic sign: which letters split Ukrainian from Russian?
fields.back: І, Ї, Є, Ґ mean Ukrainian. Ы, Э, Ё mean Russian.
fields.extra: Belarusian keeps І but adds the unique Ў.

fields.front: Cyrillic in the Balkans: **Ђ and Ћ** vs **Ѓ and Ќ**?
fields.back: Ђ and Ћ: Serbian. Ѓ and Ќ: Macedonian.
fields.extra: Љ, Њ, and Џ appear in both. Serbia mixes Latin and Cyrillic on its signage.

fields.front: **Σ, Δ, Λ, Ω** on signs
fields.extra: Greece or Cyprus; lowercase Greek is softer and rounder than Cyrillic.

fields.front: A **horizontal bar runs along the top of every word**. Which scripts and countries?
fields.back: Devanagari or Bengali: India, Nepal, and Bangladesh
fields.extra: Bengali letterforms lean triangular under the bar; Devanagari strokes stay straighter.

fields.front: Tibetan-style lettering on mountain-road signage. Which country?
fields.extra: On Street View these letters only appear in Bhutan.

fields.front: Hebrew vs Arabic in one look?
fields.back: Hebrew: boxy, disconnected letters. Arabic: flowing, connected script.
fields.extra: Urdu in Pakistan resembles Arabic but with softer, curvier fonts.

fields.front: The street sign says **iela** vs **gatvė** vs **tänav**. Which country is each?
fields.back: iela: Latvia. gatvė: Lithuania. tänav: Estonia.
fields.extra: Abbreviations: g. in Lithuania, tn in Estonia. Estonian tee means road.

fields.front: Street words: **rue, calle, rua, via**?
fields.back: rue: French. calle: Spanish. rua: Portuguese. via: Italian.
fields.extra: Handy when the rest of the sign is just names.

fields.front: Street words: **utca, ulica, sokak**?
fields.back: utca: Hungarian. ulica: Polish and South Slavic. sokak or cadde: Turkish.
fields.extra: utca is a strong Hungary tell since nothing nearby resembles Hungarian.

fields.front: Street words: **straat** vs **Straße**?
fields.extra: Switzerland writes Strasse with ss. A blue 'fietspad' sign is Dutch for bicycle path, not a street name.

fields.front: Internet domain **.ch**
fields.extra: Confoederatio Helvetica, Latin for the Swiss Confederation. Not China, which is .cn.

fields.front: Internet domain **.za**
fields.extra: From Zuid-Afrika.

fields.front: Domains **.ee** vs **.ie**?
fields.extra: Eesti is Estonia's own name for itself.

fields.front: Domains **.at** vs **.au**?
fields.extra: Driving side breaks any tie: Austria right, Australia left.

fields.front: Domains **.dk** vs **.de**?
fields.extra: Æ and Ø on the same sign confirm Denmark.

fields.front: Domains **.my** vs **.id**?
fields.extra: The languages look similar; plates (two vs three blur sections) and road lines split them.

fields.front: Internet domain **.hr**
fields.extra: From Hrvatska, Croatia's own name for itself.

fields.front: Domains **.si** vs **.sk** vs **.se**?
fields.extra: Slovenia and Slovakia trip everyone up; the yellow directional signs are Slovenia's.

fields.front: **Reddish-pink bicycle lanes** flanking almost every road. Which country?
fields.extra: Cyclists everywhere confirm it, in a landscape flat to the horizon.

fields.front: **Deep red soil** along a tropical highway. Which country do you think of first?
fields.extra: Paraguay and Argentina's Misiones province share the red dirt, as do patches of Africa and Australia.

fields.front: A red diamond-shaped sign with a **white circle** in the countryside. Which country?
fields.extra: They mark hunting grounds and are a famous Portugal-only tell.

fields.front: Brutally patched, crumbling asphalt in an Eastern European toss-up. Which way do you lean?
fields.extra: Russian roads are generally kept somewhat better; confirm with the alphabet (І, Ї, Є mean Ukraine).

fields.front: Small **blue rectangular plates** mounted on utility poles. Which country?
fields.extra: The community calls them stickers; they are unique to France.

fields.front: An Indonesian round with **tiled roofs in patchy colours**. Which island?
fields.extra: The most useful single region tell in the world's fourth most populous country.

fields.front: White roadside flowers in a Baltic toss-up: real meta or fake meta?
fields.back: Semi-fake: the flowers grow across all the Baltics but are most common in Estonia, especially in Gen 3.
fields.extra: Use it as a tiebreaker, never as proof. Fake metas are clues the community spread that are not fully true.

fields.front: The game mode **NMPZ**
fields.extra: One static view per round, so car metas and sky clues matter far less; the purist duel format.
